data.title: Novo Nordisk: The Market Has Lost Its Mind
data.description:
Article Thesis

Novo Nordisk A/S (NVO) reported its quarterly earnings results on Wednesday, Aug. 6th, showing ongoing appealing growth thanks to its weight-loss drug and its diabetes franchise. And yet, NVO's shares fell, following an already massive share price decline over the previous couple of months. Novo Nordisk is now a lot cheaper than it used to be, even before the weight-loss drug business started taking off, and its dividend yield is now (almost) at an all-time high. For a company with an excellent track record and a positive growth outlook, that does not seem logical, I believe—the market is underpricing NVO now, whereas it overpriced NVO during the peak of the Wegovy hype. I believe that buying now will pay off handsomely for long-term-oriented investors.

Past Coverage

I have written about Novo Nordisk several times here on Seeking Alpha in the past, which included Buy ratings before the weight-loss drug hype and a less bullish rating during the weight-loss drug hype. My most recent article is from this spring, when I gave Novo Nordisk a "Buy" rating. So far, this has not worked out, as shares continued to pull back. But I believe that the underlying growth and value story remains intact—NVO has gotten even cheaper. With Novo Nordisk announcing its Q2 results on Wednesday, it is time to take another look at this biopharma giant today.

What Happened?

On Wednesday morning, Novo Nordisk A/S reported its Q2 earnings results. These were the headline numbers:

    • Novo Nordisk press release (NVO): 1H GAAP EPADR of DKK 12.49.

    • Revenue of DKK 154.94B (+16.1% Y/Y).

Yes, growth was down compared to a year ago, when Novo Nordisk grew even more explosively. But despite growth slowing down to some extent, growth remained excellent, at 16%. Most biopharma companies, especially large ones like Novo Nordisk, grow not at all or only by a couple of percentage points. That's not surprising, as patent expirations, pressure to reduce drug costs, and the law of large numbers (nothing can grow at a very high pace forever) make it difficult to grow at a hefty pace beyond a certain point. And yet, NVO is delivering a mid-teens growth rate, whereas companies like Bristol-Myers Squibb (BMY) grow at a low single-digit pace only. I thus believe that a decline in NVO's growth rate shouldn't be overinterpreted—growth still is excellent on an absolute basis and on a relative basis, compared to other big pharma players, and it was clear that NVO would not grow at 20%+ forever. Let's take a deeper look at how the company has performed so far this year.

Novo Nordisk: Way More Than Just An Obesity Play

While Novo Nordisk has gotten a lot of attention for its obesity/weight-loss drug Wegovy in recent years, Novo Nordisk is much more than a single-drug company. Wegovy isn't even NVO's biggest drug, and weight management isn't Novo Nordisk's biggest franchise.

Instead, Novo Nordisk has, for many years, been focused on diabetes care. Here, the majority of NVO's revenues are generated, even today. NVO's diabetes business grew by 8% during the first half of the year, relative to one year earlier, adjusted for currency rate movements. This franchise includes GLP-1 drugs, mainly Ozempic, which showed appealing growth of 15%, as well as human insulin, long-lasting insulin, fast-acting insulin, and so on. Around two-thirds of Novo Nordisk's revenues are generated by its diabetes segment.

The next segment is what Novo Nordisk calls obesity care, which mainly consists of Wegovy, while Saxenda also contributes some revenues, although with a negative growth rate. Wegovy's strong growth has allowed the obesity care segment to grow by a very strong 58% compared to one year earlier during the first half of the current year, backing out currency rate movements.

Novo Nordisk's third segment is its rare diseases portfolio, which is smaller than the other two, but which is showing appealing growth as well: Revenues here were up by 15% so far this year, relative to one year earlier. Novo Nordisk's rare diseases business is not very large relative to the rest of the company, but it provides some nice diversification, and its growth is appealing (and not dependent on the rest of the company's portfolio).

While Novo Nordisk grew its revenues by 16% so far in 2025, its profit growth was considerably better. This is due to operating leverage, as Novo Nordisk's costs do not grow as fast as its revenues. Research and development costs, for example, are not tied to the revenues that the company generates and do not grow much when sales are rising. Operating leverage thus allows for margin expansion as the company grows, which is why Novo Nordisk's operating profit grew by a very nice 25% during a time when revenues rose by a still pretty nice, although not quite as nice, 16% (operating profit was up by 29% in constant currencies). A somewhat higher tax rate caused net income to grow a little less than operating profit, but net profits were up by 22% as well on a reported basis (and more on a currency-neutral basis).

While Novo Nordisk won't grow quite as much this year as it had thought at the beginning of the year, its outlook for 2025 is still pretty positive. The company guides for double-digit revenue growth, at around 11%, while operating profit is forecast to grow by 13%. The lower guidance, relative to half a year ago, is caused by competition for its Wegovy weight-loss drug stemming from compounded versions of this drug. But that's a problem that Novo Nordisk is tackling—the company has just started to move against compounders more intensely. The threat of lawsuits by a deep-pocketed company like Novo Nordisk should make at least some of the companies that compound Wegovy move away from this business model, and NVO may even get some court wins. While one can criticize management for ignoring this issue for too long, I like that the company is doing more to get rid of this competitive threat going forward, and I would not be surprised to see Wegovy's growth improve a couple of months ago as legal battles against drug compounders progress.

Beyond 2025, Novo Nordisk's growth outlook is positive as well. Analysts are forecasting revenue growth of around 10% in 2026, 2027, and 2028, which would be well above the industry average. Since Novo Nordisk is working on next-generation weight-loss drugs, such as CagriSema (which has recently shown very solid results in a trial), the obesity care segment could continue to grow at a nice pace for many years, I believe. Novo Nordisk also seeks to expand the usage of semaglutide, which is the active ingredient in Wegovy, in other areas, such as liver disease MASH (metabolic dysfunction-associated steatohepatitis). There are thus several growth vectors for Novo Nordisk, while its core diabetes care business should continue to deliver solid growth as well, thanks to a steadily growing market and NVO's excellent market position.

Before the recent hefty growth caused by NVO's success with Wegovy, Novo Nordisk grew by roughly 10% per year for many years, which seems achievable in the future, too. And yet, the market prices NVO as if its business were to perform way weaker in the future, relative to the past:

Novo Nordisk now trades at just 12x forward earnings, while it has, on average, traded at twice that valuation in the past. This would make sense if its growth over the coming years would be way weaker compared to the growth five or ten years ago, but that is, I believe, unlikely. While NVO will likely not grow by 20%-30% per year going forward, it didn't deliver such a growth rate during most of the past either. And yet, the market was willing to pay a 25x earnings multiple for a quality company growing at roughly 10% per year back then. Today, NVO trades at a way lower valuation, making it a pretty great value on a relative basis.

The very low valuation has also made NVO's dividend yield spike, as we can see in the following chart:

While investors oftentimes got a 1%-2% dividend yield when they bought in the past, they now get one of the highest yields ever—the dividend yield was only higher than today for a very short time during the initial COVID crash.

Is NVO A Good Investment?

Due to its strong position in the growing diabetes market, NVO has been a solid investment for many years. Its growth was not outrageous, but better than the big pharma average. I believe that this will hold true in the future, too, and many analysts agree, forecasting appealing business growth for NVO in 2025 and beyond.

Novo Nordisk now trades at less than one-third its price from a little more than a year ago, as the market has irrationally sold off NVO due to some temporary headwinds from drug compounding (NVO will still deliver double-digit growth this year). With NVO trading at less than half the long-term valuation average and with its shares now offering a pretty strong dividend yield, I believe it's a good time to buy into this quality company—the market is acting irrationally right now and offers a gift to long-term oriented investors.

data.title: VICI Properties: You Should At Least Consider The 5.1% Yield For Inflation Protection (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Introduction

With the market currently down over 500 points at the time of writing after reciprocal tariffs went into effect, the market is likely coming to grips with inflation potentially remaining higher for much longer.

And with more uncertainty likely in the coming months, buying into a company with a 5.1% yield doesn't sound like a bad idea, especially if of high quality.

With VICI Properties (NYSE:VICI), investors get a dividend yield over 5% that's well-covered to go along with more upside potential over the long-term. But due to their recent price appreciation, substantial upside may be limited near-term, reason for my downgrade from buy to a hold.

In this article I discuss VICI Properties' latest earnings, fundamentals, and why they could be still considered attractive.

Previous Thesis

I last covered VICI Properties back in May in an article titled: Near-Term Upside Likely Limited, But Still A Solid Bet Long-Term.

Since, VICI has performed well, holding up even better than I anticipated. The REIT is up a little over 6%, besting the S&P (SP500) who's up 5.78% over the same period.

I continued to rate VICI a buy due to their strong fundamentals, robust liquidity, and their then 5.5% dividend yield as I suspected they could hold up well during market volatility and increased economic uncertainty.

During Q1, VICI missed FFO estimates and declined from $0.57 to $0.51 the year prior. Revenue posted a solid beat by $7.17 million, amounting to $984.2 million.

Despite the FFO miss management raised full-year AFFO guidance. Their income stability and double-digit upside to their price target of $36.21 made them a solid bet long-term.

Strong Performance Amidst Uncertainty

As previously mentioned, VICI remains one of my core portfolio holdings, mainly for their income stability and upside potential. In their latest Q2 last week, VICI continued their strong performance despite a tough economic backdrop.

Revenue reached $1 billion, up from the previous quarter's $984.2 million, and 4.6% from the previous year's $957 million. AFFO also climbed $0.03 from the previous quarter to $0.60. Year-over-year, this represented a 4.9% increase on a per share basis.

In total, growth was slightly higher at 6.4%, AFFO growing from $592.4 million to $630.2 million. As a result of their performance, management again raised full-year AFFO guidance to $2.35 - $2.37, up from $2.33 - $2.36.This represents a 4.4% growth rate from 2024. Again, solid considering the macro environment.

Investment Activity

As far as investment activity, it was a fairly quiet quarter, but the REIT still found ways to produce capital through internal funding. And while some investors may worry about VICI providing funding to tenants, this has provided attractive returns for shareholders.

VICI's management touched on this during earnings:

To date in 2025, we are generating our earnings growth through a combination of same-store earnings growth and new store external growth. When it comes to same-store earnings growth, VICI's owners benefit from a same-store NOI growth rate that, according to Green Street's latest published net lease research is over 5x higher than the average projected rate of same-store NOI growth for net lease REITs. What you are seeing through this internally funded growth, are the advantages of VICI having achieved our current level of scale with more than $600 million in retained cash flow available for investment.

VICI provided a $510 million loan for the development of North Fork Mono, a casino & resort near Modera, California. The hotel is managed by Red Rock Resorts (RRR). This is part of the partnership the two companies announced back in May.

Moreover, with limited casinos in the state of California, the partnership opens up growth opportunities for both, particularly for VICI Properties. They also increased their previous investments in the One Beverly Hills development to $450 million, up from $300 million.

This is part of the previous announced partnership with Cain International and Eldridge Industries, which I touched on in my prior article. And going forward, I expect VICI to look for additional partnerships to drive future growth opportunities.

Balance Sheet

VICI also managed to deleverage the balance sheet, making good on their promise in the prior year's quarter. Net debt to EBITDA stood at 5.1x, down from 5.4x and within management's targeted range of 5.0x - 5.5x. Closing on their bond offering, the REIT has no debt maturing until the second half of 2026.

There's also a good chance of VICI refinancing at a lower rate as these both have weighted-average interest rates of 4.5% and 4.25% respectively.

Liquidity was plentiful with $621.5 million in forward equity after settling roughly 9.7 million shares for $296 million in net proceeds, which management used some to repay their maturing debt.

They also had $233 million in cash, bringing total liquidity to $2.9 billion. Total debt was $17.1 billion, basically flat year-over-year.

5.1% Yield Offers Inflation Protection

Although inflation has remained sticky, VICI's current 5.1% yield offers protection, likely the reason they've experienced upside in recent months and help up during volatility.

Moreover, with AFFO guidance raised on a per share and total basis, the dividend remains well-covered. The REIT should also be announcing a dividend increase soon which I'm expecting a new quarterly dividend between $0.45 - $0.46 a share. And you can see since inception, their dividend has been well-covered.

Management also mentioned dividends and inflation during earnings saying that dividend-paying stocks could rise in the future as investors look to income stocks as a result of sticky inflation risks and an aging population.

Additionally, while some readers discuss VICI's disappointing share price returns in recent years, their total returns since the beginning of 2019, the year after they IPO'd, is near identical to the S&P.

Limited Upside

Once interest rates come down and there's more clarity regarding the state of the economy, I expect VICI to break out, at least above the $35 level. With their new guidance, this gives VICI a forward P/AFFO multiple of 14.22x currently.

Using their normal multiple of 15.68x according to Fast Graphs, VICI's end of 2026 price target offers solid upside to their price target of $38.25. While I do think VICI deserves to trade at least around 15x - 16x, their upside will be highly dependent on the direction of interest rates.

Additionally, due to their recent price appreciation, investors should look to add to VICI on any share price weakness, particularly near the $30 level.

Risks

Although VICI has inflation protection in their annual rent escalators to mitigate some risks, the slowing economy which has impacted Las Vegas does have an impact on their operators.

If we continue to see the economy slow, their operators could run into financial issues, although I expect them to continue covering their full rent.

But this could result in potential downside or limited upside as a large portion of VICI's rent roll comes from the Las Vegas Strip with Caesars Entertainment (CZR) accounting for 39%.

Management also mentioned a slowdown in Canadian travel and gaming, but stated they were confident in the long-term trajectory of both Las Vegas and Canada.

Investor Takeaway

While VICI Properties may not see significant upside in the near term, the REIT still remans attractive due to their well-covered 5.1% yield and potential to see tailwinds from lower interest rates.

Additionally, their internal growth structure and size & scale has allowed them to grow earnings during heightened uncertainty, a testament to management and their fundamentals.

Moreover, with inflation likely to remain sticky due to tariffs, VICI's income reliability and price stability during a volatile market makes them an attractive option for income investors currently.

data.title: Embotelladora Andina Is Recovering And Fairly Valued
data.description:
Embotelladora Andina (NYSE:AKO.B) (NYSE:AKO.A) recently released its 2Q25 results.

I haven't reviewed the name in almost a year. Over that time, the company's business has improved, mainly in Argentina, and in part, following the dynamic of the Chilean stock market, the share has appreciated more than 20%.

In this article, I use a different approach to value the name, not based on simple earnings but also on available cash flow and growth. I find that the name can provide a return of about 11/12%, not particularly attractive given the cyclical position, and therefore maintain a Hold rating.

Coming from a challenging place

Last time I wrote about Andina, the company was facing challenges in most of the countries where it operates.

In particular, in Argentina, the economic adjustment carried by the new government led to a tremendous drop in volumes and profitability, which is only moderately being recovered so far. In Brazil and Chile, persistent inflation and high rates moderate the pace of income growth and pricing power, and in all countries, depreciation of the currency impacted on costs.

Some of those factors have reverted in FY25 YTD, as evidenced in the company's results.

Argentina's better, albeit expensive

Starting with Argentina, sales have improved significantly, almost 25% in 2Q25 and YTD. 2Q24 was probably the worst point of the economic adjustment, and therefore, the cyclical recovery is stronger. During the call (so far only in Spanish in audio format), management said that the company has already recovered 75% of the ground lost in 2024, and that going forward, the recovery will be more moderate.

In fact, it was Argentina the driver of most of the revenue growth of the company in the quarter, and of all of its profitability. When we compare CLP revenues and EBITDA, 60% of the sales increase (CLP 70 billion) came from Argentina (CLP 40 billion), and the totality of EBITDA (CLP 10 billion).

When talking of CLP figures, though, and as I always comment on any foreign company operating in Argentina, we need to be sure to consider that Argentina is becoming more expensive in USD and CLP terms because inflation is running hotter than the rate of depreciation. That means Argentina becomes more profitable in units and aggregate terms. The problem, as always with Argentina, is that these dynamics are seldom sustainable, and usually the country goes from very expensive to very cheap in foreign currency.

That means, in my opinion, that the aggregate profitability of the country is probably a little lower, albeit estimating how much is hard. Argentina's multi-lateral real exchange rate had appreciated by 20% between 2Q24 and 2Q25. Part of that has already reverted in 3Q25.

More pricing power in Chile and Brazil, albeit depreciated

In Brazil and Chile, the company recovered pricing capacity, being able to grow revenues above volumes in local currency, and passing some of the effect of inflation (MSD in both countries) to consumers.

The offset of this was higher costs coming from depreciated currencies.

Some of the company's largest costs are dollar-denominated, namely the concentrate bought from Coca-Cola (KO), sugar (outside of Brazil), and PET resins used for the bottling plants.

Coca-Cola, in part, offsets the effect of depreciation on the price of concentrate. During the call, some of this was discussed around the distribution agreement. We can also note in the results the comment that concentrate prices decrease,d offsetting the depreciation of the local currencies.

In Brazil, volumes were up only 1% but sales were up 9% in local currency, because of pricing power capacity. However, costs were up 13% because of the depreciation, and therefore, BRL's profitability fell.

In Chile, volumes were better, at 3.5%, and pricing added another 5 percentage points of growth in sales. In this case, costs were in line with sales, at 8.5%.

A cold year

In all of the company's markets, winter has been significantly colder than in previous years. This obviously impacts the consumption of cold beverages, which are the company's staples. This had an impact in 2Q25, and will probably have an impact in 3Q25 (which includes the winter months of July and August in the southern Hemisphere).

The company did not provide a figure for this impact, though, but we can expect that under other conditions, sales would have been even better.

No big changes ahead

All of this talks of a context in which demand, albeit not booming, has recovered.

The company does not expect a significant improvement or worsening in any of its markets. It expects LSD growth in Chile through this year (which is electoral), with little volatility after the change of government, considering the relatively conservative political outlook in Chile. In Brazil, the company expects that conditions will remain positive for consumer products, as the government puts a focus on poverty alleviation. Finally, in Argentina, it expects continued recover,y albeit more moderate. There were no comments on the call about the FX reversal risk.

A cash and growth valuation model

Throughout all the time I have been covering Andina (first article in 2021), I have used a P/E valuation approach, focusing on the long-term profitability of the company.

Although this method is valid, I want to delve one step deeper in this article, and separate earnings from cash available for shareholder returns (dividends), and also account for growth potential.

Starting with profitability across the cycle, I believe using an average of 12% in EBIT margins is conservative, instead of using the current figures, where profitability margins are higher, mainly because of Argentina's higher productivity.

At 12% EBIT margin and $3.5 billion in revenues, we can expect the company to generate EBIT of about $420 million across the cycle. From this, we need to remove approximately $50 million in interest expenses and 30% in taxes to reach $260 million in earnings.

So far, there's no difference from a classical P/E approach. However, in order to add growth and actual returns, we need a few more assumptions.

On the returns side, I expect that the company's leverage (assets to equity) levels will remain more or less constant, and that asset utilization will not continue to grow. This will allow me to assume that the company will require to finance growth in equity (CAPEX above D&A and working capital) in line with earnings growth.

For growth, one simplifying assumptions are flat margins over time. This is logical after assuming a cycle-average level for EBIT margin, and a consistent leverage level (given that debt has to move up with equity to keep leverage constant).

Another assumption is that the P/E ratio remains constant. This is needed for earnings growth to become a shareholder return via share appreciation. We could assume (bullishly) that the multiple can expand, but I prefer to remain conservative.

Finally, I believe the name can grow revenues in USD at about 5% over the long-term, this is based in moderate LSD growth in the countries where it operates, plus another LSD addition from inflation over time globally.

Based on this, therefore, we get a yield of 5% only from the growth. From earnings ($260 million), we need to remove the cost of growth, that is, 5% of equity ($1.1 billion, or $55 million). That means the company would have about $205 million to distribute to shareholders. Indeed, this figure is consistent with long-term payout ratios.

Finally, we need the market cap. Embotelladora has two classes of shares, and in most databases, the company therefore shows two different market caps. However, based on the share data (page 18 of the recent release), we know that there are 76.5 million ADS of each class, meaning an actual market cap of about $3.3 billion.

This means the company offers a potential cash flow distributable yield of 6.6%, plus growth of 5%. This is a total of about 11/12%.

However, the shares have different rights; the B shares receive 10% more dividends than the A shares. After some equations, we can therefore find that B shares have rights to $97.5 million ($1.28 per ADS) if the $205 million were paid as dividends, and the B shares have rights to $107.5 million ($1.4 per ADS).

That means the A shares have a potential dividend yield of 7% and the B shares one of 6%. The A shares are therefore slightly more attractive at this valuation.

I think, however, that 11% or 12% are not particularly attractive for a name exposed to emerging economies, with the potential for a tactical reversal in earnings in one of its largest markets (Argentina), and under the risk of earnings depreciation (in USD).

I believe the name is fairly priced, albeit not a steal. I therefore maintain a Hold rating on AKO

data.title: Davis Financial Fund 2025 Semi-Annual Review
data.description:
The average annual total returns for Davis Financial Fund’s Class A shares for periods ending June 30, 2025, including a maximum 4.75% sales charge, are: 1 year, 26.77%; 5 years, 20.40%; and 10 years, 10.65%. The performance presented represents past performance and is not a guarantee of future results. Total return assumes reinvestment of dividends and capital gain distributions. Investment return and principal value will vary so that, when redeemed, an investor’s shares may be worth more or less than their original cost. For most recent month-end performance, click here or call 800-279-0279. Current performance may be lower or higher than the performance quoted. The total annual operating expense ratio for Class A shares as of the most recent prospectus was 0.94%. The total annual operating expense ratio may vary in future years. Returns and expenses for other classes of shares will vary.

This material includes candid statements and observations regarding investment strategies, individual securities, and economic and market conditions; however, there is no guarantee that these statements, opinions or forecasts will prove to be correct. All fund performance discussed within this material refers to Class A shares without a sales charge and are as of 6/30/25 unless otherwise noted. This is not a recommendation to buy, sell or hold any specific security. Past performance is not a guarantee of future results. There is no guarantee that the Fund performance will be positive as equity markets are volatile and an investor may lose money.

Strategy: Growth in Disguise

Since its inception in 1991, Davis Financial Fund (DFF) has invested in durable, well-managed financial services companies at value prices which the fund could hold for the long term. Shelby Cullom Davis’s quip that financial services companies can be “growth companies in disguise” remains a bedrock tenet of our approach. Investors tend to place low valuations on financial companies because of their earnings volatility. However, many financial companies generate capital through the business cycle at an attractive rate which they use to pay dividends, buy back stock or otherwise deploy in ways that increase shareholder value. By focusing on economic reality rather than investor sentiment, DFF has compounded shareholder wealth at +11.73% annually over 34 years, outpacing both the S&P 500 Index and the S&P 500 Financials Index.

First-Half Results: There and Back Again

The S&P 500 Index returned +6.20% in the first half of 2025. The proverbial casual investor who only checks their 401(k) balance on occasion might be forgiven for concluding that it was an uneventful six months. The reality of course is far different. Among other things, the U.S. became embroiled in a trade war—initially focused on China, Canada and Mexico but widened globally on Liberation Day—whose outcome remains uncertain, but which could reasonably upend longstanding supply chains and depress consumer demand. There were dramatic moves in long-term U.S. Treasury yields both up and down (though ultimately the 10-year Treasury yield ended the first half more than 30 basis points lower than it was at the start of the year). Conflict in the Middle East re-escalated. The S&P 500 Index saw a −19% peak-to-trough drawdown during the period, although prices had fully recovered by the end of the first half.

With a +9.23% return, the S&P Financials Index outperformed the S&P 500 Index in the first six months of 2025. The strongest performers were selected banks (notably JPMorgan Chase (JPM), Citigroup (C) and Wells Fargo (WFC)), investment banks, exchanges and consumer finance companies. Alternative asset managers, selected payments companies and regional banks lagged. The financials index experienced a similar steep drawdown (and recovery) as the broader index. Financial companies—and regional banks in particular—don’t have significant direct exposure to tariffs but obviously could experience a second-order impact in the form of rising credit losses and a slowdown in loan growth. However, the −23% drawdown in the S&P Banks Select Industry Index during the first half seemed overdone to us. It was another illustration of our long-running contention that memories of the 2008–09 financial crisis still weigh on investors, making them leery of financial stocks.

As noted, DFF outperformed the S&P Financials Index with a return of +13.77%. The biggest contributors to our relative performance were our foreign banks (Danske Bank (OTCPK:DNSKF)(OTCPK:DNKEY), DNB Bank (OTCPK:DNBBF), Metro Bank (OTCPK:MTRBF) and DBS Group (OTCPK:DBSDF)(OTCPK:DBSDY)), selected U.S. banks (JPMorgan Chase and Wells Fargo), Capital One (COF) and capital markets companies (BNY Mellon and Schwab). The fund’s largest detractors were U.S. regional banks (U.S. Bancorp (USB), Fifth Third (FITB) and PNC Financial (PNC)).

Market Environment: Business Developments

We disclose in these letters the market performance of the fund and relevant benchmarks. But we also like to reference Ben Graham’s famous observation: “In the short run, the market is a voting machine, but in the long run, it is a weighing machine.” We are most concerned with how the intrinsic value of our companies develops, and accept that there will be periods (sometimes quite lengthy ones) where market value and intrinsic value diverge. The market performance of the fund and the S&P Financials Index over one-, three- and five-year periods is quite likely to have exceeded the growth in intrinsic value. However, the longer-term returns seem to represent intrinsic value more closely.

It is often difficult to observe the rate of change in intrinsic value over short periods of time such as a year or six months. The signal can easily be drowned out by the noise. However, we think the recent period has been notable for the number of significant events at core fund holdings that could be positive catalysts for growth in intrinsic value.

Capital One closed its acquisition of Discover Financial in May of this year. This is a transformative deal for the company in which the pro forma ownership is split approximately 60/40 between Capital One and Discover shareholders. Such large deals often give us pause but, in this case, we are comforted by our trust in the Capital One management team led by Richard Fairbank, the reasonable relative valuation between the two stocks, and the strategic logic of the deal. In addition to targeting cost synergies of $1.5 billion, management is anticipating so-called “network” synergies of $1.2 billion from transitioning certain Capital One debit and credit card volumes into Discover’s networks. Importantly, this synergy target only envisions a minority of Capital One’s credit card volume transitions. Longer-term, though, we think the company has an opportunity to continue integrating its card-issuing activities with its card network. There will undoubtedly be noise in their near-term results—both from integration costs and accounting oddities. However, looking out a few years we believe Capital One remains attractively priced at less than 10x earnings despite the potential, in our view, to earn +/−20% returns on tangible capital on average and over time.

Wells Fargo was released (after seven years) from the asset cap that the Federal Reserve imposed on it. It has been difficult to accurately gauge what impact the asset cap had on the bank’s ability to service customers. The bank had avenues to attract and retain the most attractive parts of a client’s business (from a return-on-assets perspective). But inevitably Wells Fargo must have been losing out at the margin, and with the passage of time and the underlying growth in the core parts of their client franchise, the asset cap surely had begun to bite. Getting out from under the asset cap will undoubtedly be positive for the bank from a client service and revenue perspective. Just as significant, we think, is the signal from the Federal Reserve that Wells Fargo is well on its way to improving its compliance and controls infrastructure. It can be difficult from the outside to point to exactly where in the income statement this improvement will appear, but it is an important milestone in the bank’s march to its medium-term target of 15% return on tangible equity (and beyond).

Markel Group is a property and casualty insurer that carries a significant amount of excess capital allocated to a portfolio of public equities and controlling interests in private, middle-market companies (leading some to call it a “mini-Berkshire Hathaway”). During the first half, it announced that its board would conduct a review of its businesses. This followed the emergence late last year of a so-called activist investor who advocated for the sale (most likely to a leveraged private equity fund) of the company’s portfolio of wholly owned, well-run, non-insurance businesses. In our view, such a sale would be a serious mistake for two reasons. First, given that private equity firms create value by buying undervalued businesses, it stands to reason that selling undervalued businesses to them would destroy value for Markel shareholders. Second, this portfolio of diversified companies adds a welcome third engine to Markel’s growth and stability, allowing them to absorb the volatility inherent in insurance underwriting cycles and the short-term swings in investment returns. As long-term shareholders, we wholly endorse this three-pronged strategy that has been tested and proven over the last two decades. Given Markel’s extraordinary culture of stewardship and candid communication with shareholders, we expect the vast majority of other long-term shareholders endorse it as well. That said, we do think that the involvement of an activist investor aligns with our thesis that there is a meaningful gap between the company’s stock price and its intrinsic value.

Rocket Companies (RKT), the nation’s largest mortgage originator, announced two notable acquisitions in the first half of the year—Mr. Cooper (COOP), the largest U.S. mortgage servicer, and Redfin, a leading digital real estate brokerage firm. Beyond the financial rationale for the deals, management has a vision for fundamentally streamlining customers’ home ownership experience. Existing Rocket shareholders will own approximately 70% of the pro forma company once the dust clears. As we noted with respect to Capital One above, we do get nervous when companies engage in such adventures. In the case of Rocket, we take comfort from the oversight and alignment of interests of Rocket’s founder and controlling shareholder, Dan Gilbert.

The pace at which the gap between market price and intrinsic value will close is beyond our control. Danske Bank, which was among the strongest drivers of fund performance so far this year, is a good illustration. We first purchased Danske Bank for the fund in 2018. At the time, the bank was under a cloud owing to violations of anti-money laundering rules by its Estonian subsidiary. In the ensuing years, Danske had to undergo comprehensive change in the management team, settle a large monetary penalty with regulators, and commit significant resources to enhancing its risks and controls infrastructure. While some of these milestones were achieved a few years back, the stock valuation only began to “re-rate” over the last year or so. With Danske stock up +35% in its local currency in the first half, the gap to intrinsic value has continued to narrow, though we still believe the shares are reasonably priced at 1.3x tangible book value.

We could not possibly end a discussion of the noteworthy developments at our portfolio companies without acknowledging Warren Buffett’s decision to hand over the CEO position at Berkshire Hathaway to Greg Abel at the end of this year. Berkshire’s compounded annual return over Warren’s tenure from 1965 through 2024 was 19.9%, compared to 10.4% for the S&P 500 Index.1 Moreover, Berkshire (BRK.A)(BRK.B) stock outperformed the index in 46 out of 56 annual rolling five-year periods, and in 43 out of 51 annual rolling 10-year periods. Size undoubtedly played a role in narrowing the potential for outperformance of Berkshire stock as time progressed, a point Warren himself has been making since at least 1982. However, the consistency in the company’s performance is staggering. As shareholders, we are grateful for Warren’s stewardship, and as students we are grateful for his patient teaching.

Valuation: Dispersion Continues

We noted above that stock prices have increased faster than intrinsic value has grown in the last five years. This largely reflects just how low stock prices were at the starting point in June 2020—not quite the market depths of COVID, but still dire. Today we continue to believe our companies’ valuations are sufficiently low that they should generate attractive returns over time from here.

While index valuations—both the broader market index and the S&P Financials Index—seem high by historical comparison, both are still heavily influenced by the dispersion in valuations. The dispersion in the S&P 500 Index caused by the Mag 7+1 companies (see Figure 2) is generally known. They collectively trade for 32.5x earnings. There is also dispersion within the S&P Financials Index. Approximately 35% of the companies in the S&P Financials Index (weighted by market capitalization) traded at a multiple higher than 21x 2025 forward earnings at the end of the first half, and their weighted average P/E multiple was 28.9x.2 Included in this group were the payment networks, alternative asset managers, financial data companies, financial software providers, insurance brokers and exchanges. Many of these are very high-quality companies whose business models and management teams we admire. Stocks trading at such valuations can still be very compelling investments depending on their growth trajectory and the economics of their business, in which case we would happily be (and are) shareholders. Each of these companies has a story, and some of them might even come true. However, in our experience, it would be extremely difficult for the vast majority of these companies to all grow into such a rich valuation.

DFF’s holdings skew toward the types of businesses that make up the other 65% of the S&P Financials Index, namely U.S. money center and regional banks, consumer finance companies, property and casualty insurers, wealth managers and foreign banks. These companies are more balance sheet-intensive and should earn lower returns on capital. They bear credit and underwriting risk so their returns can be lumpy (though that isn’t necessarily negative: regular readers of these letters may recall that we often say we prefer “a lumpy fifteen to a smooth twelve”). They are regulated. These companies should trade at a valuation discount but with the fund’s weighted average P/E multiple estimated at approximately 13.1x,3 we believe both that the discount is too wide and that the prospective absolute returns on our portfolio from here should be more than satisfactory.

Outlook: Durable Across Outcomes

We remain consistent in our approach to allocating capital in our portfolio—that is, we look for companies with durable competitive advantages coupled with competent and honest managements that are priced at a discount to their intrinsic value. We invest presuming that we will own our companies through business cycles. We do not attempt to build a portfolio around a particular speculative forecast—for example, by trying to predict where interest rates or the economy will go. Rather, we strive to construct a portfolio that will perform well over the long term across a range of economic outcomes. As such, our portfolio is diversified across leading franchises earning above-average returns on capital in banking, payments, custody, wealth management, and property and casualty insurance. We remain excited by the investment prospects for the companies in Davis Financial Fund.

For more than 50 years we have navigated a constantly changing investment landscape guided by one North Star: to grow the value of the funds entrusted to us. We are pleased to have achieved strong results thus far and look forward to the decades ahead. With more than $2 billion of our own money invested in our portfolios, we stand shoulder to shoulder with our clients on this long journey.5 We are grateful for your trust and are well-positioned for the future.

data.title: How China's EV Industry Is Shaping The Global Market: Insights From The Founder Of China's Top EV Blog
data.description:
The rapid rise of electric vehicles (EVs) in China is playing an increasingly influential role in the global automotive landscape, capturing the attention of investors, policymakers, and consumers worldwide. At KraneShares, we’ve seen firsthand how innovation in China’s EV sector is powering growth and investment opportunities, which is why we launched the KraneShares Electric Vehicles & Future Mobility Index ETF (KARS) in January 2018. KARS is designed to capture global companies that are driving the mobility ecosystem, with a healthy allocation to China's top companies like battery manufacturer CATL and EV maker BYD (OTCPK:BYDDF).

To gain deeper insight into this dynamic market, Brendan Ahern, CIO at KraneShares, sat down with Phate Zhang, founder of CnEVPost, China’s leading English-language news source on new energy vehicles (NEVs). Their conversation highlighted the driving forces behind the growth of China’s EV industry and the opportunities and challenges on the horizon for those investing in the space.

Q&A with Phate Zhang, Founder of CnEVPost

Q: What’s your background, and can you tell us about CnEVPost?

I graduated from the University of International Business and Economics (UIBE) in Beijing in 2009 with a degree in finance. After graduation, I joined China Daily, writing English news about China's economy and companies for two years, then moved to Reuters’ Beijing office for another two years. In 2013, I relocated to Shanghai and spent a year in corporate public relations at a state-owned company, but continued working in media, including six years at Wallstreetcn, where I covered the Federal Reserve and other central banks, White House policies, and international markets.

CnEVPost was created by chance: during the National Day holiday in October 2019, I bought a year of cloud services and set up a blog called CnTechPost to share news about China’s tech industry and products in my free time. As China’s EV industry expanded, EV content became a significant part of my posts, leading to the launch of CnEVPost at the end of 2020, focused solely on EV-related news. In April 2021, I left my full-time job to devote myself to these sites, and a few months later, I shut down CnTechPost to concentrate entirely on CnEVPost.

Q: What are the main factors driving China’s rapid EV adoption compared to the US and Europe?

I believe this is mainly due to government support, a well-established supply chain, and automakers quickly responding to consumer demand with products that meet their needs. While there have also been government stimulus policies in the US and Europe, EV adoption hasn’t grown as fast in those regions as in China. I think this is because of a lack of disruptive innovation from local companies, which has slowed local supply chain development and limited consumer options.

Over the past five years, innovation in the EV industry has been almost weekly, if not daily, in China. This includes stronger powertrains, improved designs, faster charging, and more advanced software, making EVs seem like the next generation of cars compared to traditional gas-powered vehicles for many people. Once you’ve driven one and gotten used to the experience, it is sometimes difficult to go back to traditional gas-powered cars.

Only Tesla (TSLA) currently offers such products in Europe and the US. Traditional automakers are moving cautiously and slowly, while Chinese startups are more nimble.

Q: I don’t think BYD’s achievements receive the attention and respect they deserve. What are your thoughts on the company and its founder?

I believe this is normal, as brand building and changes in public perception take time. BYD has been around for just 30 years, and its rise in the NEV sector has only happened in recent years. Before 2021, even in China, BYD was seen as a lower-end brand and not the first choice for car buyers. Only through the launch of numerous competitive new models over the past few years has BYD achieved its current position in China and globally.

Building brand awareness might take even longer in international markets. Established automakers naturally enjoy more trust. Additionally, many people still doubt products from Chinese companies, and shifting this mindset will also take time.

BYD chairman Wang Chuanfu is a leader who has directed the company to accumulate a wealth of technology over the past 30 years and deployed it strategically. The disruptive innovations enabled by these technologies have caught traditional automakers off guard, laying the groundwork for BYD’s rapid rise.

Q: How important are government policies and subsidies in shaping China's EV market?

I think government policies can be decisive, but they are not enough alone. These subsidies allow consumers to buy EVs that offer a potentially enhanced experience than traditional cars at lower prices. China’s subsidies for the EV industry apply to all brands, including Tesla, Volkswagen (OTCPK:VWAGY), and Ford (F). Domestic automakers, especially startups, have been quicker to act and respond to shifting consumer preferences, which has helped them quickly gain ground.

Traditional domestic automakers have been slow but are catching up fast, launching new brands and competitive models. Foreign automakers have been caught off guard by the rapid market changes, losing some market share. If they take China’s EV market seriously, they can still succeed. For example, the N7 sedan recently launched by Dongfeng Nissan, led by its local Chinese team, has received good reviews.

Q: With over 100 EV brands in China, how do you see the market consolidating over the next five years, and which companies are most likely to survive?

I believe that about half of these EV brands, many of which are nearly unknown, may disappear in the next five years. Still, the well-known brands are likely to remain relatively safe. Currently, most industry experts recognize only about 50 to 60 EV brands. These brands should be fairly secure. It wouldn’t be surprising if many lesser-known brands vanished in the fierce competition ahead.

The companies that will survive are those with strong brand recognition, unique positioning, and their own technologies.

Q: How intense is the price war among Chinese EV makers, and how does it affect profitability and innovation?

Unlike previous years, this year’s price competition in China’s EV market is more subtle. Few companies have directly cut prices to join a price war, except for BYD and a few others in late May.

Our observation is that EV manufacturers have been updating products frequently this year to improve user experience and lower prices, which boosts sales. Leapmotor and XPeng (XPEV) are prime examples, as both have refreshed many models this year, leading to significant growth.

In terms of profit, lowering prices through product updates is more sustainable than outright price cuts on existing models, though price reductions still hurt profits. At the same time, this pushes automakers to ramp up innovation, as they must meet increasingly selective consumer preferences.

Q: Reuters published an excellent article on how Chinese EV makers are adapting differently from other automakers. Is this mainly due to China’s manufacturing expertise, or are there other factors?

Yes, that’s a great article. Aside from leveraging China’s manufacturing supply chain, I see the quick actions by Chinese automakers and their pursuit of success. I believe international car companies also want to do the same, but face more constraints. Currently, Chinese automakers tend to be smaller, which gives them more room for innovation. This may be the dilemma of innovators.

Q: EV gets a lot of attention, though the correct term should be NEV, which includes EV and hybrids. When I met with BYD last year, they discussed their hybrid tech at length. Do you have any thoughts on EV versus hybrids in China?

China’s city-level licensing restrictions complicate this topic. For instance, Shanghai has been providing free license plates for years to buyers of battery EVs (BEVs) and plug-in hybrid EVs (PHEVs), while buyers of traditional internal combustion engine cars need to pay about RMB 90,000 in an auction for a license plate. Starting in 2023, Shanghai no longer offers free plates for PHEVs.

Beijing enforces stricter rules, treating PHEVs the same as gasoline cars. Buyers of BEVs must also secure license plate quotas in advance, often taking years to do so. Guangzhou and Shenzhen have different policies.

These policies have made BEVs more popular in China’s big cities. Lower-cost hybrid models are still more common outside these areas due to lower income levels and less developed charging infrastructure. Overall, the share of BEVs in NEV sales has been steadily falling, from over 80% in early 2021 to about 60% now, according to data from the China Passenger Car Association (CPCA).

I believe that unless China’s policies change significantly, PHEVs (including extended range EVs known as EREVs) will continue to grow, mainly driven by demand from second and third-tier cities and less developed regions.

Q: What are the main risks and opportunities for investors eyeing China’s NEV sector over the next five years?

While I don’t offer investment advice, I can share some personal insights. For the NEV industry and all others, the key for investors is to focus on incremental changes rather than just the current state of the industry.

When evaluating a specific company, investors should ask if it can keep growing at its current pace, whether there are risks they haven’t seen, and if new businesses or market shifts could unexpectedly impact performance—up or down.

Additionally, international macroeconomic and geopolitical factors, especially Federal Reserve policies, require close attention. Having spent six years focused on global macroeconomics and market reporting, I believe liquidity in international markets often heavily influences the valuation of Chinese assets.

Conclusion

Chinese automakers and battery suppliers have emerged as influential players in automotive technology, manufacturing scale, and cost competitiveness. Their rapid expansion, driven by aggressive export strategies, pioneering R&D, and investments in overseas production, has contributed to the transition to clean transportation far beyond China’s borders. As international competition intensifies, China’s advancements are likely to dictate how the global auto industry pivots to a more sustainable and innovative future.

The KraneShares Electric Vehicles & Future Mobility Index ETF provides investors with access to many companies in China's dynamic EV sector and to other global companies engaged in the production of EVs and/or their components.

This material contains the speakers’ opinions and third-party projections. It is provided for informational purposes only and should not be regarded as investment advice or a recommendation of specific securities. Holdings are subject to change. Securities mentioned do not make up the entire portfolio and, in the aggregate, may represent a small percentage of the fund. While the discussion may highlight potential opportunities in a particular sector or strategy, investors should also consider the inherent risks associated with any investment—including economic, regulatory, market, and geopolitical uncertainties—that may affect performance and outcomes. A balanced evaluation of both risks and potential rewards is essential when considering any investment.

Definitions:

BEVs (Battery Electric Vehicles): vehicles powered entirely by electricity stored in rechargeable batteries, with no internal combustion engine or gasoline use; they rely solely on electric motors for propulsion and are charged by plugging into the electric grid.

PHEVs (Plug-in Hybrid Electric Vehicles): vehicles that combine an internal combustion engine with an electric motor and a battery that can be recharged by plugging into an external power source; they can be driven short distances using only electricity, after which the gasoline engine takes over to extend range.

EREVs (Extended Range Electric Vehicles): vehicles powered primarily by electricity from a battery and an electric motor, similar to BEVs. However, unlike BEVs, EREVs include a small internal combustion engine (ICE) that acts only as a generator to recharge the battery when it is low, not to drive the wheels directly. This configuration is sometimes called a “series hybrid” because the engine’s only function is to generate electricity, not to provide mechanical propulsion.

data.title: Yum! Brands: Growth And Liquidity Are Tasty, But Overvaluation Spoils Upside Potential
data.description:
Yum! Brands, Inc. (NYSE:YUM) closed the first-half with sustained strength despite the stubborn inflation and stiff competition. Its solid revenue growth and stable margins remain one of its main attractions. This is supported by its strategic business model and robust Balance Sheet, promising sustainability and dividend longevity. However, entering at this current price remains challenging. In addition, its bearish signals are still evident today.

YUM: Tasty Sales Growth, But Spoilt Margins

Yum! Brands, Inc. just released its Q2 2025 results earlier today, and we can say that it’s still doing fine despite the headwinds it faces. Its operating revenue amounted to $1.93B, up by 9.7% YoY from $1.76B. All its revenue components rose, showing a stronger performance in both restaurant and franchising segments, mainly from KFC and Taco Bell.

KFC remains the core revenue growth driver as it comprises over 50% of the total number of YUM-related restaurants. Its system sales increased by 6.0% YoY to $8.72B, faster than the increase in its restaurant count. In fact, KFC generated average sales of $269,424 ($8.72B/32,369) per restaurant versus $268,044 ($8.22B/30,689). This shows that its continued expansion remains fruitful. As a result, YUM derived more franchise fees from KFC franchisees.

The same goes for Taco Bell as its system sales rose by 6.4% YoY to $4.28B. This is the highest sales growth among its restaurants. Each Taco Bell restaurant generated average sales of $488,236, a 4.1% YoY increase from $469,002. With that, we can say that Taco Bell was the most productive restaurant in the group. It’s not surprising that franchise fees also rose to $248M YoY.

However, Pizza Hut and Habit Burger are still having a hard time coping with the challenging market. Somehow, this was expected, especially for Pizza Hut, considering the fierce pizza competition with Domino’s (DPZ) and Papa John’s (PZZA). DPZ dominates the pizza market, particularly the US market. Meanwhile, PZZA is still smaller but increasing in global presence. Pizza Hut appears to have a shrinking presence in the US market. Aside from that, its restaurant count decreased by -96. Even if it reduced its restaurants, its average sales per restaurant weakened from $158,074 to $157,626 YoY. Despite its positive growth adjusted to forex, we can’t deny that Pizza Hut still struggles to regain its footing.

In terms of revenue growth, it is also much better than in Q2 2024 at only 4.5% YoY. Yet, it is important to note that revenue growth appears to be weakening again from Q4 2024. This may have been influenced by various factors, such as inflation. It remains stubborn considering its rebound to 2.4-2.7% coupled with the rate cut pause. This still signals uncertainty, which affects discretionary spending. After all, positive inflation means the general price level is still increasing.

We can also see its impact on operating costs and expenses as the combined amount rose by 13.4% YoY. This completely offset revenue growth. Aside from that, its operating leverage rose from 42.6% to 45.5%, which means that YUM is more cost-sensitive today. As a result, its operating margin decreased from 34.4% to 32.2% YoY.

Why YUM Is Still Strong

Despite the weaker performance of Pizza Hut and higher operating leverage, YUM remains a high-margin business, which is crucial in the current macroeconomic environment. Having a 30% operating margin means a lot to cover its whole operations even without resorting to borrowing. One important aspect to consider is the fact that a substantial portion of its operations is dedicated to franchising, which is less capital-intensive and more resilient to inflation. So, it’s not surprising that it maintains a robust cash flow. In fact, its operating cash flow is enough to cover all investing and financing expenditures, excluding dividend payouts and share repurchases. This means that YUM remains very liquid and sustainable. Aside from that, it generated an FCF of $708M during the first half. This means that YUM converted 19.0% of its revenue into free cash using the FCF/Sales Ratio. This is higher than in the H1 2024 with 17.9%. More interestingly, its actual FCF in Q2 of $374M is also better than in my previous coverage last Q1 at only $313M. Hence, its current operations have higher cash returns both on a YoY and QoQ basis, making it more fruitful today.

YUM complements it with its prudent debt management. It still relies on it at some level, but it continues to repay maturing debt on time. As a result, the total amount remains relatively stable. It’s still high, but YUM will cover it. In fact, its Net Debt/EBITDA keeps decreasing. It’s now 3.8x versus my first and second coverage at 4.1x and 3.9x, respectively. This means that YUM generates more cash and earnings while keeping borrowings stable.

Valuation

To assess it, I updated my P/E Ratio considering the high sensitivity of the stock price to the EPS. Currently, YUM trades at 28.74x its EPS, which is more expensive than the five-year average of only 26.01x. This is also the second-highest P/E since 2020. If we use the average to derive its fair value, the TP will be $132.11. This is quite higher than in my previous coverage at $131.87. Yet, its overvaluation remains evident.

I also made an update on its DCF Model. I increased its Perpetual Growth Rate to 2.0% because I think it’s now reasonable for its recent performance. It’s also more consistent with the US inflation. Meanwhile, I estimated WACC 7.17% using the capital asset pricing model. Doing this is quite straightforward since most of the numbers can be gathered. For instance, the market value of equity is its market capitalization. For the market value of debt, I simply copied its most recent debt and the interest expense as the cost of debt. I also set its risk premium at 5.0% to compensate for the risk to be taken in YUM instead of just enjoying the 10-year treasury yield. For its Beta, I simply computed and compared the five-year daily price change of YUM relative to the S&P 500 (SPX) using the SLOPE function on MS Excel. To be sure, you can just copy the Beta reported on reliable websites like Yahoo Finance, which is 0.73. A Beta lower than 1.00 means that YUM is less volatile than the S&P 500. To be more conservative, I just used 0.76 from my computation.

In the DCF Model, I only updated the value of cash, debt, minority interest, and the number of shares. In the end, I came up with $132.08 as my TP. This is much lower than $172.76 in my previous model. I think my new TP is more precise since this is close to my price valuation using P/E.

Technicals

YUM has early bearish signals, although more indications may be needed to verify this. As we can see, the price has been generally decreasing in the past four weeks. Now, the price has already crossed below the 50-day and 200-day SMAs. In addition, the 50-day SMA has flattened, which shows a potential shift in market sentiments. To confirm this, the price must break below its first key support of $138.42. If not, the downtrend will not hold long. The 50-day SMA must also cross below the 200-day SMA. Considering the current price and SMA pattern, I advise investors to stay on the sidelines.

Meanwhile, MACD reflects its weak momentum. The MACD line and histogram are in negative territory, which shows that more bears are moving in the market than bulls. It also means that selling volume still exceeds buying volume. Its RSI can explain this because the stock became overbought right before the correction. This proves the recent overpricing. As a result, profit-takers entered and pushed the price down. At this point, there is now enough room for reentering at this price. Yet, it will be better to wait for RSI to hit 30 or become nearly oversold. At our TP of $132, it is possible that the sell-off has already stopped. So, holding rather than buying is a safe choice.

Conclusion

Yum! Brands, Inc. remains resilient with KFC and Taco Bell at the forefront, supported by its strategic business model to stabilize revenue growth and capital requirements. Liquidity remains impressive with its robust operating cash flow coupled with prudent debt management. However, entering can be risky amid its overpricing. Technical risks are also emerging, although more confirmations are still needed. I am reiterating my hold rating on this stock.
